full_text:
ESCALATION IN THE MIDDLE EAST AND BEYOND — MOBILITY REPORT
Data collection period: 12 - 18 May 2026 | Publication date: 21 May 2026
OVERVIEW
The escalation of hostilities across the Middle East and beyond has triggered a humanitarian crisis spanning 
multiple regions in an already fragile context, with significant impact to population mobility. National social 
systems and host community safety networks are under considerable strain as protection needs continue to rise. 
The temporary cessation of hostilities remains fragile and subject to change, requiring continued monitoring and 
operational readiness while the humanitarian consequences of earlier hostilities continue to affect populations 
across multiple regions.
In the Islamic Republic of Iran, widespread destruction of more than 149,000 civilian infrastructures and 
essential services,1 including hospitals, schools and emergency facilities, continues to reduce basic living conditions. 
As of 29 April 2026, an estimated 400,000 people have seen their homes directly affected by housing 
damages. Mobility patterns are being shaped by housing damage, livelihood loss, and service disruptions, leading 
to temporary internal relocation, reliance on family networks, and increased labour related movement. While 
no large scale cross border displacement has been observed, the cumulative pressures are contributing to 
heightened interest in outward migration among vulnerable groups, including youth, migrants, and low income 
urban households. Overall, population movements remain localised, temporary, and economically driven, 
with no signs of mass displacement; however, ongoing strain on housing, livelihoods, and basic services is 
likely to sustain elevated mobility levels in the near- and medium-term. 
In Lebanon, despite the announcement of a new 45-day ceasefire on 15 May, hostilities persist. As security 
conditions worsens, displacement remains severe, with over one million internally displaced persons (IDPs) 
reported,2 including 129,721 IDPs accommodated across 632 collective sites3 though a majority remain 
outside formal sites. Geographic distribution of displacement is uneven – areas such as Beirut, Mount Lebanon 
and North Lebanon are experiencing mounting pressure from successive waves of displacement, while returns 
remain limited due to insecurity, damaged housing, lack of services, and risks associated with unexploded ordnance. 
Collective sites and host communities face sustained pressure as some previously displaced families return to 
shelters after unsuccessful attempts to return home. Cross-border flows into the Syrian Arab Republic are 
continuing, with a total of 333,856 movements recorded as of 18 May 2026,4 placing additional pressure on 
an already overstretched humanitarian system.
IOM’s Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) is on the ground, actively tracking displacement and cross-border 
mobility while scaling operations in coordination with local authorities and partners to better understand how 
the crisis is impacting human mobility in the region.
1 Source: Iranian Red Crescent Society, April 2026.
2 United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, (May, 2026). Lebanon: Flash Update #23 – Escalation of hostilities in Lebanon (as of 4 
May 2026).
3 Source: Lebanon Disaster Risk Management Unit, May 2026.
4 Cross-border figure into the Syrian Arab Republic are now sourced from DTM Syria, in coordination with border authorities in the country as of 18 May 
2026 while cross-border figure into Lebanon are from Lebanon Border Control Committee (BCC), in collaboration with the General Directorate of General 
Security (GSD) and the Land Border Regiments), as of 20 April 2026. Please note that due to variations in population coverage, sources and methodology, 
official sources on movements between Lebanon and Syrian Arab Republic may differ..
5 Cumulative flow data from the Islamic Republic of Iran to both Afghanistan and Pakistan are reported for the period from 01 March to 18 May 2026. Data for 
movements between the Islamic Republic of Iran and Iraq are available between 01 and 30 April 2026 for 4 out of 12 BCPs. Lebanon–Syrian Arab Republic 
figures are reported separately for 02 March to 30 April 2026. Sources include DTM, the Lebanon Border Control Committee (BCC) and General Security 
Directorate (GSD), the Iraq Border Point Commission, and Pakistani authorities. Increases and decreases noted below are week to week changes.
6 These PoEs represent a subset of the 43 official land PoEs located along the borders of the Islamic Republic of Iran, the Syrian Arab Republic, and Lebanon.
7. This BCP is located on the Syrian Arab Republic side of the border with Lebanon.

IMPACT ON NEIGHBOURING COUNTRIES 
OF THE ISLAMIC REPUBLIC OF IRAN
The conflict is affecting mobility along shared borders between the Islamic Republic of Iran and its neighbouring 
countries, driving reactive movements and precautionary travel within and across borders. 
Since the start of the crisis, flows from the Islamic Republic of Iran to neighbouring countries have 
fluctuated over time, varying significantly between countries. Afghanistan receives the highest number of 
movements with substantial dips and spikes while Pakistan and Iraq see much smaller and more occasional 
movements. Overall, cross-border flows remain moderate and variable rather than steadily increasing.
AFGHANISTAN 
Between 01 March and 18 May 2026, a total of 358,496 individuals entered Afghanistan from the Islamic 
Republic of Iran, corresponding to a daily average of 4,538 movements, while 141,023 individuals departed 
Afghanistan to the Islamic Republic of Iran over the same reporting period (1,785 daily average).
During the reporting period of 12-18 May 2026, cross-border movements along the Afghanistan–Islamic 
Republic of Iran border at Milak, Islam Qala, and Abu Nasr Farahi border crossing points (BCPs) remained 
stable, with all three BCPs fully operational and no disruptions reported. 
Both inflows from the Islamic Republic of Iran to Afghanistan and outflows from Afghanistan to the Islamic 
Republic of Iran continued at a consistent level, in line with the trend observed during the previous week.
Inflows continued to be shaped by a mix of forced and voluntary returns with forced returns outweighing 
spontaneous returns. Reported drivers included police harassment, limited livelihood opportunities, and 
rising living costs in the Islamic Republic of Iran. This is particularly notable among inflows recorded at Malik 
BCP, with a higher volume of voluntary returns by Afghan passport holders with Iranian visas returning. This 
is reportedly due to unpaid wages, limited employment opportunities, approaching Eid celebration in the 
Islamic Republic of Iran.
Analysis of Flow Monitoring Survey (FMS) found that from 12 to 18 May 2026, outflows of Iranian nationals 
were predominantly recorded via Islam Qala–Taybad, with intended destinations concentrated in Sistan and 
Baluchestan (approximately 35%), Razavi Khorasan (23%), and T ehran (16%). The main drivers reported for 
leaving the previous country prior to transiting Afghanistan included family reunification in the Islamic Republic 
of Iran (around 40%), security concerns (34%), and visa expiry or non-renewal (28%). Nearly all respondents 
(approximately 99%) cited family, relatives, or social networks in the Islamic Republic of Iran as the primary 
reason for selecting their destination. 
In contrast, inflows of Iranian nationals from the Islamic Republic of Iran into Afghanistan were overwhelmingly 
recorded via Islam Qala–Taybad (approximately 96%), with final destinations mainly in Herat (48%), followed 
by Kabul (13%) and Balkh (9%). These movements were predominantly individual-based (approximately 89% 
traveling alone) and short-term, with 83% intending to stay less than one month. Reported motivations were 
largely economic or temporary in nature. Meanwhile, return movements of Afghan nationals continue to 
reflect ongoing economic hardship and protection pressures within the Islamic Republic of Iran, reinforcing 
sustained mixed and circular mobility dynamics across the border.
3
ESCALATION IN THE MIDDLE EAST AND BEYOND — MOBILITY REPORT
Data collection period: 12 - 18 May 2026 | Publication date: 21 May 2026
PAKISTAN
Between 01 March and 18 May 2026, a total of 22,673 
individuals crossed from the Islamic Republic of Iran 
into Pakistan, representing a daily average of 287 flows. 
Movements occurred primarily through the Taftan–Mirjaveh 
crossing (19,222 individuals) and the Gabd–Kumb–Rimdan 
crossing 3,451 individuals).8
Cross-border movement from the Islamic Republic of Iran to 
Pakistan through the Gabd and Taftan BCPs remains restricted. 
Currently, only students, drivers, pilgrims, and tourists with 
valid passports and visas are permitted entry. 
Many travellers are using Pakistan solely as a transit route 
due to the closure of Iranian airports amid the conflict, 
before continuing to onward destinations. Those traveling 
irregularly to the Islamic Republic of Iran are detained by Iranian 
authorities and handed over to Pakistan’s Federal Investigation 
Agency (FIA) for processing.
Border authorities reported no influx of Iranian nationals 
other than those with valid passports and visas in Pakistan. 
The majority of Iranian arrivals subsequently returned to 
the Islamic Republic of Iran, primarily as their visits were 
trade-related. 
Outbound movement from Pakistan to the Islamic Republic 
of Iran continues on a limited basis, restricted to Pakistani 
students and drivers. Although many students initially returned 
to Pakistan at the onset of the conflict, some are now traveling 
back to the Islamic Republic of Iran to resume studies. 
As of 18 May 2026, a total of 68 Third Country Nationals 
(TCNs) were recorded in Pakistan. According to authorities, 
TCNs entering through the Taftan and Gabd–Rimdan borders 
were mostly tourists or businessmen. Many had been stranded 
in the Islamic Republic of Iran and, due to the unavailability of 
flights, transited through Pakistan before returning to their 
countries of origin.
8 Source: Pakistani authorities, May 2026.
Map 2. Mixed Population Movements between the Islamic Republic of Iran, Afghanistan and Pakistan (as of 18 May)

ESCALATION IN THE MIDDLE EAST AND BEYOND — MOBILITY REPORT
Data collection period: 12 - 18 May 2026 | Publication date: 21 May 2026
IRAQ
CROSS-BORDER MOVEMENT
While no new movement data was received during the current reporting period (12 -18 May 2026), 
the most recent official figures from the Iraqi Border Point Commission, covering the period between 
01 and 30 April 2026, recorded a total of 271,751 individuals entering Iraq from the Islamic Republic 
of Iran, while 270,142 departed, resulting in a net incoming movements of 1,609 individuals.
As of 18 May 2026, border conditions remained broadly stable compared with the previous reporting 
period (week ending 11 May 2026), with all crossing points open and no significant restrictions observed. 
Despite the resumption of regular operations, Key Informants (KIs) reported that travel volumes have 
not yet returned to pre-escalation levels. Flows during this reporting week largely mirrored those of 
the previous week, with notable upticks recorded at Hajj Omran and Zurbatiyah. Hajj Omran BCP has 
reportedly nearly approached near-full operating conditions: Students are returning to universities in 
the Islamic Republic of Iran, medical travel is picking up, and commercial activity is regaining momentum.
No BCPs reported emergency response or security incidents during the reporting period. 
DTM FLOW MONITORING ACTIVITIES AT KEY BORDER CROSSING POINTS (BCPs) 
Between 12 and 18 May 2026, Flow Monitoring access remained restricted to Bashmagh, where 
DTM teams recorded 2,072 incoming and 2,084 outgoing movements, a 47 per cent rise in incoming 
movements and a 12 per cent drop in outgoing movements compared with the previous reporting week.
Iranian nationals dominated both directions, accounting for 74 per cent of incoming movements 
and 73 per cent of outgoing movements, with no movements of other Third Country Nationals 
(TCNs) recorded through the crossing.
Incoming movements of Iranian nationals were driven primarily by family visits (43%), employment (33%), 
and tourism (12%), with no movements reported as conflict or insecurity-related during the period. 
Outgoing movements largely reflected returns to the Islamic Republic of Iran following long-term stays 
of three months or more (66%) or short-term stays of less than three months (30%).
9 Please note that these figures represent only 4 of the 12 BCPs for which data is currently available: Muntheriyah, Al Sheeb, Al Shalamcha, and Zurbatiyah.

ESCALATION IN THE MIDDLE EAST AND BEYOND — MOBILITY REPORT
Data collection period: 12 - 18 May 2026 | Publication date: 21 May 2026
ARMENIA
No new data on movements was received during this reporting period (12-18 May 2026). 
Between 28 February and 10 March 2026, a total of 4,930 individuals entered Armenia through the 
Agarak BCP, including 4,381 Iranian nationals. The main nationalities of TCNs included the Russian 
Federation (72), the People’s Republic of China (61), Kazakhstan (43), and Germany (38). As of 
01 April, 1,262 Iranian nationals were reported to have returned to the Islamic Republic of Iran via 
Armenia from the United Arab Emirates. 
Overall, flows of TCNs follows regular mobility patterns, with no sustained abnormal increases 
observed.
AZERBAIJAN
No new data on movements was received during this reporting period (05 -11 May 2026). 
Between 28 February and 29 April 2026, a total of 3,564 individuals crossed from the Islamic Republic 
of Iran into Azerbaijan via the Astara border crossing, including 622 Azerbaijani nationals. Other top 
nationalities included People’s Republic of China (742), the Russian Federation (397), India (294) and 
Bangladesh (198). 
Overall, flows at the border between Azerbaijan and the Islamic Republic of Iran remain stable, 
with no indications of large-scale or mass crossings.
REPUBLIC OF TÜRKİYE
No new data on movements was received during this reporting period (12–18 May 2026). 
As of 18 May, the situation along the Republic of Türkiye's border with the Islamic Republic of Iran 
remains calm, with no indications of irregular movements or increased pressure. Turkish authorities 
during this period signalled a continued focus on maintaining regional stability and supporting 
diplomatic efforts to reduce tensions in the wider region.
TURKMENISTAN
No new data on movements was received during this reporting period (12–18 May 2026). 
As of 06 May, Uzbekistan organized the return of 41 Uzbek citizens from the Islamic Republic 
of Iran through Turkmenistan. This is in addition to 13 Uzbek citizens evacuated earlier through 
Turkmenistan.
According to the EU Delegation in Turkmenistan, additional evacuations of TCNs have taken place. 
These reportedly included one citizen each from Finland, Austria, Sweden, and France, as well as two 
citizens from the Netherlands. 
10 The reporting period for these figures was not specified.

IMPACT BEYOND THE ISLAMIC REPUBLIC 
OF IRAN
The escalation of hostilities is affecting mobility well beyond the borders of the Islamic Republic of 
Iran, with the widening conflict influencing movement patterns across multiple regions. 
LEBANON
CROSS-BORDER MOVEMENT
No new data on cross-border movements was received during this reporting period (12 - 18 
May 2026). 
Between 02 March and 20 April 2026, there were 326,992 individuals crossing from the Syrian 
Arab Republic into Lebanon.11 The majority of those crossing were Syrian nationals (80%), followed 
by Lebanese (17%) and other nationalities (3%). 
INTERNAL DISPLACEMENT
Since 02 March 2026, hostilities across Lebanon have continued to intensify, prompting population 
movements in response to growing insecurity, widespread damage to infrastructure, and overstretched 
welfare services. 
The humanitarian situation is deteriorating rapidly, triggering large scale and repeated displacement, 
as well as onward movements with little notice.
On 15 May, a new 45-day ceasefire extension was announced; however, hostilities persisted, 
with continued strikes reported across southern Lebanon governorates. Following the 
deterioration in security conditions, displacement remain high, with over one million 
internally displaced persons (IDPs) reported, 13 including 129,721 IDPs accommodated 
across 632 collective sites though a majority remain outside formal sites. 12 
This represents a 7 per cent increase compared to the previous week, when 121,701 IDPs 
were recorded in collective sites. Geographic distribution of displacement is uneven with areas 
such as Beirut, Mount Lebanon and North Lebanon are experiencing mounting pressure from 
successive waves of displacement,, while returns remain limited due to insecurity, damaged housing, 
lack of services, and risks associated with unexploded ordnance.14
11 Source: Lebanon Border Control Committee (BCC), in collaboration with the General Directorate of General Security (GSD) and the Land 
Border Regiments). 
12 United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, (May, 2026). Lebanon: Flash Update #23 – Escalation of hostilities in 
Lebanon (as of 4 May 2026).
13 Source: Lebanon Disaster Risk Management Unit, May 2026.
14 United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, (May, 2026). Lebanon: Flash Update #27 – Escalation of hostilities in 
Lebanon (as of 18 May 2026)

ESCALATION IN THE MIDDLE EAST AND BEYOND — MOBILITY REPORT
Data collection period: 12 - 18 May 2026 | Publication date: 21 May 2026
SYRIAN ARAB REPUBLIC
As of 18 May, a total of 333,856 movements had been 
recorded along the four points of entry (PoEs) at the 
Syrian- Lebanese border where DTM Syria tracks 
population movements into the Syrian Arab Republic. 
Most movements recorded involved Syrian nationals 
(96%), with Lebanese nationals accounting for the 
remaining 4 per cent.
In addition to cross-border movements into the Syrian 
Arab Republic, DTM Syria implemented Emergency 
Mobility Tracking (EMT) to capture arrivals into the 
country. Between 02 March and 18 May 2026, DTM 
Syria recorded 122,083 individuals arriving across 
1,230 locations in all 14 governorates in the Syria Arab 
Republic. Those recorded at arrival locations were 
predominantly Syrian nationals (99%), with Lebanese 
nationals accounting for less than 1 per cent.
Between the 12 – 18 May 2026, Key informants (KIs) 
reported that arriving returnees continue to face multiple 
reintegration challenges. Access to safe housing remains 
a primary concern, with many residing in damaged or 
partially destroyed homes, unfinished buildings, temporary 
shelters, or low-quality rented accommodation. 
Children’s educational integration continues to pose 
challenges, especially for those who have already been 
enrolled in Lebanese curriculum, resulting in difficulty 
adapting to the Syrian education system. 
Additionally, livelihoods remain precarious and 
overwhelmingly informal, leaving returnees largely 
dependent on seasonal agricultural work and low-paid 
daily labour, and few find employment commensurate 
with their prior experiences and skills. A saturated labour 
market, compounded by weak demands for workers 
has driven wages down and heightened exposure to 
exploitative employment practices. 
Most arrivals were adult men (31%), followed by adult 
women (25%), girls (22%), and boys (21%).15 A distribution 
that likely reflects employment-related drivers, including 
both access to livelihoods and job losses. Among the 
arrivals currently employed (44% of locations), the majority 
relied on daily wage labour (79%), followed by salaried 
work (39%) and agriculture or livestock activities (39%). 
Notably, most KIs considered available income to be 
inadequate, with 52 per cent reporting it was not sufficient 
at all to meet basic needs and 46 per cent indicating it 
was only partially sufficient. 
Such insufficient, unstable, and short-term income sources 
may push individuals to adopt negative coping strategies 
to meet basic needs, while also contributing to risks of 
secondary displacement. The continued reliance of some 
employed arrivals on remittances (18%) and humanitarian 
assistance (4%) further underscores that income alone is 
not sufficient to achieve financial stability.
15 Sum of percentages adds up to less 100% due to rounding. 
Figure 9. T op reported perceived issues and risks faced

Challenges 
Thirty-four per cent of KIs identified significant 
challenges affecting returnee population (up from 
32% in Round 10, indicative of a worsening situation 
for the returnee population). 
Psychosocial distress was the most frequently reported 
issue (76%), largely linked to financial hardship, 
limited employment opportunities, and rising living 
costs. Barriers to accessing essential services were 
reported by 53 per cent of KIs, while 43 per cent 
highlighted challenges related to lost or damaged 
legal documentation (Please see Figure 9 for further 
breakdown).
Protection risks were most pronounced in Al-
Hasakeh (100%), followed by As-Sweida (79%) and 
Rural Damascus (61%).

DISCLAIMER
The opinions expressed in the report are those of the authors and do not 
necessarily reflect the views of the International Organization for Migration 
(IOM). Requests for commercial use or further rights and licensing should 
be submitted to dtmsupport@iom.int .
The following citation is required when using any data and information included 
in this information product: “International Organization for Migration (IOM), 
Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM), May 2026. Escalation in the Middle East 
and Beyond — Mobility Report”. 
For more information on terms and conditions of DTM reports and information 
products, please refer to: https://dtm.iom.int/terms-and-conditions
METHODOLOGY
This analysis is based on information compiled weekly from IOM’s 
country offices. DTM monitors population mobility and displacement 
dynamics across countries in Afghanistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Iraq, 
Lebanon, Pakistan, the Republic of Türkiye, Turkmenistan, and the 
Syrian Arab Republic.
Data is drawn from primary DTM flow monitoring and mobility tracking 
activities, alongside secondary sources, such as government data, with all 
secondary information validated by our field teams. This includes data on 
cross-border movements, internal displacement, and qualitative insights 
on emerging mobility trends and humanitarian needs. While efforts 
are made to align reporting to the same reference week, collection 
periods may vary across countries.

related.title: Aligning the U.S. and Canadian Defense Industrial Bases
related.summary: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but  by the ability to produce at scale . After decades of  reliance on globalized supply chains  for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the  Defense Production Act  to  incentivizing private capital  investments and  even selective government equity stakes . For many countries that have pursued efficiency-driven globalization for decades, the economic pull of China’s market makes unilateral disengagement difficult, even when national security concerns are clear.
bullets: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but by the ability to produce at scale .

After decades of reliance on globalized supply chains for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the Defense Production Act to incentivizing private capital investments and even selective government equity stakes .

For many countries that have pursued efficiency-driven globalization for decades, the economic pull of China’s market makes unilateral disengagement difficult, even when national security concerns are clear.
Luckily, for many countries, allied markets represent a significantly larger combined economic opportunity than China alone.

The challenge, therefore, is not simply one of dependence, but of working together through aligned demand, investment, and industrial policy to create markets and demand signals that can compete with the gravitational pull of China’s economy.
related.excerpt: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but by the ability to produce at scale . But they cannot succeed alone — and importantly, they do not need to start from scratch. After decades of reliance on globalized supply chains for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the Defense Production Act to incentivizing private capital investments and even selective government equity stakes . Canada is undergoing a parallel shift, with increased defense spending commitments, the recent release of its first Defence Industrial Strategy , and the newly launched Defence Investment Agency . These efforts create significant opportunity, but also carry risk. While the United States and Canada already share deep and often underappreciated industrial integration, in some sectors, this collaboration is applied late or unevenly. related.title: Western Withdrawal, Jihadist Expansion: How the Sahel Became Ground Zero for Global Terrorism
related.summary: The United States completed its  withdrawa l  from Niger in September 2024, abandoning key drone and intelligence hubs after its military government terminated their bilateral agreement. On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali. French forces  withdrew  from Mali in 2022 after the military junta demanded its exit, ending Operation Barkhane.
bullets: The United States completed its withdrawa l from Niger in September 2024, abandoning key drone and intelligence hubs after its military government terminated their bilateral agreement.
On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali.
French forces withdrew from Mali in 2022 after the military junta demanded its exit, ending Operation Barkhane.

The systematic breakdown of military and diplomatic relationships with Sahelian states has left Western actors increasingly unable to see, shape, or contain events in what has become the world’s most lethal theatre of jihadist violence.
From Intervention to Expulsion Over the past three years, Western military presence and influence across the central Sahel have collapsed.
related.excerpt: On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali. Claimed by Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin , a jihadist group, and conducted in coordination with Tuareg separatist forces from the Front de libération de l’Azawad, the attacks targeted multiple nodes across the country’s security architecture simultaneously, from the capital Bamako to Gao, Mopti, and Kidal. While the attacks themselves were a shock, they should be understood as the logical endpoint of a deteriorating security trajectory that Western governments have watched from an ever-increasing distance. The geographic scope of the attacks, the targeting of senior officials, and the coordination between jihadist and separatist forces reflect a step change in insurgent capability that has been building for years. Western governments now face a predicament of their own making. As the regional security environment has worsened, their capacity to respond has diminished in direct proportion. The systematic breakdown of military and diplomatic relationships with Sahelian states has left Western actors increasingly unable to see, shape, or contain event

related.title: Israeli security minister stirs diplomatic outrage with flotilla activist abuse video
related.summary: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir posts video taunting detained Gaza flotilla activists – video Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, said the images were “unacceptable”, and demanded the release of all Italian citizens involved, along with an apology for the mistreatment and the display of “total contempt” toward the Italian government. “We condemn the actions of Israeli minister Ben-Gvir – who Australia has sanctioned – and the degrading actions of Israeli authorities towards those detained.”  New Zealand’s foreign affairs minister, Winston Peters, instructed the ministry to call in the Israeli ambassador so his “grave concerns” could be directly passed on. Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.
bullets: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir posts video taunting detained Gaza flotilla activists – video Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, said the images were “unacceptable”, and demanded the release of all Italian citizens involved, along with an apology for the mistreatment and the display of “total contempt” toward the Italian government.

“We condemn the actions of Israeli minister Ben-Gvir – who Australia has sanctioned – and the degrading actions of Israeli authorities towards those detained.” New Zealand’s foreign affairs minister, Winston Peters, instructed the ministry to call in the Israeli ambassador so his “grave concerns” could be directly passed on.

Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.
“The team reports systemic violations of due process, and widespread physical and psychological abuse by Israeli authorities,” the rights group Adalah said in a statement.
The US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, one of the country’s staunchest allies, described Ben-Gvir’s behaviour as “despicable” and said the minister had “betrayed the dignity of his nation”.
related.excerpt: Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid. Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. “The team reports systemic violations of due process, and widespread physical and psychological abuse by Israeli authorities,” the rights group Adalah said in a statement. There were “a large number of complaints of extreme violence”. Ben-Gvir’s video drew a rapid and furious response from countries whose citizens were onboard the boats, including the UK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Spain, Ireland, Australia and New Zealand, in many cases from the top of government. The US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, one of the country’s staunchest allies, described Ben-Gvir’s behaviour as “despicable” and said the minister had “betrayed the dignity of his nation”. related.excerpt: KYIV, Ukraine — Senior White House officials publicly placed Ukraine’s military ahead of allied counterparts in Europe across four venues last week, and in some respects ranked Kyiv ahead of the United States itself, even as U.S. President Donald Trump has continued to dismiss Ukraine’s military strength. The Ukrainian armed forces are “the strongest, most powerful armed forces in all of Europe,” Secretary of State Marco Rubio said last week, citing a five-to-one Russian-to-Ukrainian casualty rate and four years of battlefield adaptation. The necessity of fighting the war, Rubio said, has pushed Ukrainians to develop “new tactics, new techniques, new equipment, new technology that is creating a sort of hybrid asymmetrical warfare.” U.S. Army Secretary Dan Driscoll told the Senate Armed Services Committee the same week that Ukraine has fused drones, sensors and weapons into a single command network running across the front, while U.S. Army systems remain “compartmentalized, isolated and ineffective against modern threats.” “Ukraine’s Delta common operating system, their modular open system architecture command and control system, is absolutely incredible,” Driscoll testified. “It fu

related.title: Ukraine and Russia Are Souring on U.S. Negotiations
related.summary: Yet the Trump administration has in some ways pulled its punches when it comes to Kyiv: Although it has decreased the volume of aid it gives to Ukraine, it still sells weapons to the country via NATO, provides Kyiv with intelligence support, and sanctions Russian oil (though those sanctions have been  partially lifted ). Last August, Russia triumphantly presented a summit with the United States in Alaska as a breakthrough in its war to conquer Ukraine, later  waxing poetic  about the “spirit of Anchorage.” Almost a year later, that spirit is gone. In March, Lavrov  said  the spirit of Anchorage was evaporating; and in April, he said  negotiations  were not Moscow’s “top priority.” “It’s pretty clear they’re down on [negotiations],” said John Herbst, senior director of the Atlantic Council’s Eurasia Center.
bullets: Yet the Trump administration has in some ways pulled its punches when it comes to Kyiv: Although it has decreased the volume of aid it gives to Ukraine, it still sells weapons to the country via NATO, provides Kyiv with intelligence support, and sanctions Russian oil (though those sanctions have been partially lifted ).

related.excerpt: Senior military special operations forces leaders from the U.S., Sweden and the Philippines participated in a morning panel today about the importance of SOF partnerships and alliances, as part of Special Operations Forces Week 2026 in Tampa, Florida. During the one-hour conversation, the four participants discussed how building trust through international alliances enhances collective readiness and interoperability, making it a prerequisite for mission success in contested environments. "I think the most important thing is to continue to build trust between partners within the alliance, [and] also between partners," Swedish army Brig. Gen. Rickard Johansson, commander of Sweden's special operations forces, replied after the panel was asked what the international SOF community needs to focus on most to ensure that alliances are stronger, truly integrated and capable of meeting the challenges of an increasingly complex and contested world. "And we will continue to do that, because that is the bottom line for all activities that we are supposed to do: build trust," Johansson added. Air Force Maj. Gen. Claude K. Tudor Jr., commander of U.S. Special Operations Command Africa, concurred

related.title: Calls to Uphold International Law Dominate Security Council Debate on Civilian Protection, as Delegates Demand Safety for Journalists, Aid Workers
related.summary: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result. Decade after Resolution 2286 (2016), Medical, Humanitarian Workers under Increasing Attack In 2025 alone, she noted, the UN recorded more than 1,350 attacks on medical care across 18 conflicts. Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in  Ukraine  and infrastructure in the  United Arab Emirates .
bullets: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result.
Decade after Resolution 2286 (2016), Medical, Humanitarian Workers under Increasing Attack In 2025 alone, she noted, the UN recorded more than 1,350 attacks on medical care across 18 conflicts.
Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in Ukraine and infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates .

Rather, it results from the choice made by conflicting parties to ignore their obligations to protect civilians, to adopt increasingly permissive interpretations of international law, to subordinate the protection of civilians to claims of military necessity or to allow impunity prevail.
The Council is holding its annual day-long open debate on the protection of civilians in armed conflict as the United Nations observes the ninth iteration of its “ Protection of Civilians Week ”.
related.excerpt: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result. The Council is holding its annual day-long open debate on the protection of civilians in armed conflict as the United Nations observes the ninth iteration of its “ Protection of Civilians Week ”. Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in Ukraine and infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates . “One civilian was killed approximately every 14 minutes in 2025,” said Edem Wosornu, Director of the Crisis Response Division in the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA). All too often, civilians are not collateral damage — “they are the target”, she stressed. Explosive weapons tear through towns and cities, destroying lives and the infrastructure that sustains them. And healthcare is also under attack, as she observed that — 10 years after the adoption of resolution 2286 (2016) on protecting medical care in conflict — “the situation has only gotten worse”. related.title: Italy rethinks EU defense-financing aid as arms spending falls out of fashion
related.summary: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target. In a letter to Von der Leyen on Sunday, Meloni said it was imperative to use state cash to soften the blow of rising energy costs — and the consequent hike in the cost of living — caused by the current slowdown of oil supplies to Italy due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry.
bullets: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target.

In a letter to Von der Leyen on Sunday, Meloni said it was imperative to use state cash to soften the blow of rising energy costs — and the consequent hike in the cost of living — caused by the current slowdown of oil supplies to Italy due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz.
Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry.
He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response.
Meloni will nevertheless need to explain to NATO partners why she is considering passing on EU plans to boost defense spending at the next alliance summit in Turkey on July 7-8.
related.excerpt: ROME — Italy is considering backing out of a second EU-supported boost to defense spending, taking to about €27 billion ($31 billion) the amount of rearmament cash it could forego as it focuses on tackling soaring energy costs. Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target. Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry. He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response. The suggestion by Meloni is the second time in two months she has publicly mulled passing on an EU-driven way to push up spending. In April she suggested Italy will not take advantage of an EU scheme allowing Rome to exempt a quantity of defense spending from calculations of annual deficit spending in order to allow extra arms purchases without
